quotes.verbatim_quote:
often a #I1V° is used as a passing chord following a IV or IV7 type chord
and then leading to a I or [7 type chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the #IV° passing-chord move within blues bar 6.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
A
TURNAROUND IS ANY SERIES OF CHORDS USED IN PLACE OF AN EXTENDED
DURATION OF THE TONIC CHORD.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines turnaround terminology used through Section 18.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The last chord of a turnaround is
almost always a V7 type or it 65th (O11).
quotes.why_load_bearing: Rule for the cadential function of turnaround endings.

quotes.verbatim_quote: chromatic means going in one direction and not skipping any notes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines chromatic motion used in voice-leading analysis.

quotes.why_load_bearing: Reinterprets IVm as ii of a new dominant for a smooth turn.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The IIIm7 and/or III7 chord are used often in place of the I chord in measure 7 of blues
progressions.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Specific substitution at bar 7 of 12-bar form.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Try playing a turnaround in measures | and 2 of a blues progression
quotes.why_load_bearing: Suggests turnaround placement as an introduction.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
often referred to as “major bhues” to distinguish them from blues progressions that are based on
minor chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Distinguishes major and minor blues forms.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
widely used concept of playing a fragment

of the fundamental harmonies of a scale.

the starting chord is not that important,

but the ending chord is very important. It must fit with the chord that the fragment is being

substituted for
